Tafseer:
Remembering the upheaval of the Resurrection:
When a sentence begins with 'idha' (when), the objective is to remind of that time—meaning: remember that time, and beware of the Day when such and such will occur. This is an intensive way of reminding of that Day.
A grammatical point: 'Zilzal' is used to emphasize 'zulzilat', but its attribution to 'al-ard' (the earth) adds a specific force to the meaning. The translation is: when the earth is shaken with its [final] earthquake—meaning: it is shaken in the manner it deserves to be shaken. The actual scale of this shaking cannot be estimated today; the entire earth being shaken is an unprecedented event, but it is bound to happen; so keep it in mind, and do not live heedless of it.

The meaning of 'athqal' (burdens): 'Thiql' means burdens. Its primary reference is the dead buried in the earth, whom the earth will cast out on that Day. But the word is general, and can also refer to the treasures and secrets buried in the earth, and the records of the crimes committed on its surface. This was explained under Surah Al-Inshiqaq, verse 4: (And has cast out what was within it and become empty), and further indications will appear under Surah Al-Adiyat, verse 9: (And when what is in the graves is scattered).

Depicting human panic: This describes the effect of this terrifying situation on man, who will cry out in panic: 'What is the matter with it—that it is not stopping and is casting out everything inside!' A similar panic will affect the criminals when their records of deeds are opened, as described in Surah Al-Kahf:
(What is this book that leaves not a small thing or a great thing but that it has enumerated it?) (Al-Kahf, 18:49).

The earth will report its news: On that Day, the earth will report all the good and bad deeds performed on its surface by His command. Elsewhere, it is stated that Allah will make their limbs speak to testify against them, and even their skins will testify. In Surah Fussilat, He states:
(And they will say to their skins, 'Why have you testified against us?' They will say, 'We were made to speak by Allah, Who has made everything speak') (Fussilat, 41:21).
Whatever man does in this world, he does on this earth; therefore, it is the greatest witness against him. Just as Allah will make his limbs and skins speak on that Day, He will make the earth speak to report everyone's record.

This will be by His command: 'Because your Lord has inspired it' (bi-anna rabbaka awha laha) - 'Awha' is used here in the sense of inspiring or directing. The meaning is: the earth will do so because Allah Almighty will inspire it. This is identical to: 'We were made to speak by Allah, Who has made everything speak' in the verse cited above. It will happen by His command, and everything will be forced to obey His command. Regarding the earth, Surah Al-Inshiqaq states:
(And has responded to its Lord and was obligated [to do so]) (Al-Inshiqaq, 84:5).

Everyone will be accountable individually: 'Ashtat' means separate, individual, and alone. On that Day, people will emerge from their graves in such a state that they will have neither family, relatives, tribe, nor wealth, status, helpers, or assumed intercessors; rather, everyone will stand alone before his Lord to account for his deeds. This is detailed in Surah Maryam:
(And all of them are coming to Him on the Day of Resurrection alone) (Maryam, 19:95),
and in Surah Al-An'am:
(And indeed, you have come to Us alone as We created you the first time) (Al-An'am, 6:94).
'That they may be shown their deeds' (liyuraw a'malahum) - This states the purpose of their presentation: to see the results of their deeds in the world. Obviously, 'being shown' is used in the sense of tasting its consequence.

This details the summary. It is stated: whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it.
Note that every good and bad deed of a believer and a disbeliever will face him, but in accordance with the rule explained in the Qur'an—namely, a believer will see his minor errors, but will see that his good deeds served as their expiation; conversely, a disbeliever will see his good deeds, but will see that they were nullified by his shirk and disbelief.
After this, the rule for success and ruin is described in Surah Al-Qari'ah as:
(Then as for he whose scales are heavy [with good results], he will be in a pleasant life. But as for he whose scales are light, his home will be an abyss.) (Al-Qari'ah, 101:6-9).
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 7 and 8 was combined in the original text.]
